CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Post Office affairs as they affect Ireland require a lot
of watching. We have made our readers familiar with
the scandal of importing English officials to fill some of
the best posts in the Irish postal service. There is the
important question of stores also. Years ago we believe
the Government was compelled, or induced, by Irish
public opinion to have Government printing executed in
Treland, and the Post Office went so far as to estab-
lish separate contracts for the making of Irish postmen’s
uniforms. Are there no Irish manufacturers prepared
to tender for the supply of cloth required for these
uniforms, for the bicycles used by the telegraph messen-

ers, for tools and ofher engineering stores required by
the Post Office in Ireland? We understand that tele-
graph poles are imported from Sweden and Russia into
England ; they are creosoted in England, and sent on
to.Ireland. Why not import them direct to Ireland,
_ and have the creosoting done in this country!

Manufacturers are, we understand, required to tender
for Trish supplies delivered in London! It is true that
Mr. Buxton, the autocrat of the Post Office, in reply
to a recent question by Mr. Mecagher, stated that where
Ireland is known to be the destination of the stores, con-
tractors would be asked to tender for engineering stores
for delivery in Ireland. ~We have never heard that
Irish manufacturers were invited to tender for delivery
in Dublin except in the case of a few ladders! And what
is the use of talking about where Ireland is known to be
the destination of the stores required? THas not an
estimate to be made of what stores are required for
Ireland during any financial year? And if not, how
do the officials know the amount to include in the [Par-
liamentary Estimate? We hope this matter will be fol-
lowed up, and that evasive replies will not be accepted
as final answers.

Why should not English manufacturers be required
to tender for the delivery of Irish supplies in Ireland as
Irish manufacturers are required to tender for the deli-
very of English supplies in England? Ts not the pre-
sent system a form of ‘‘ protection’’ for England? What
has]L];e I'ree Trade ‘‘ Liberal ’ Government to say for
itself ?

There are, we learn, more than one thousand men
employed in the London Post Office factories repairing
and making stores. ~Why should not Ireland have a
share of this work? Surely a Post Office factory in Ire-
land is a reasonable demand. Again, can anyone justify
the centralisation of Irish Savings Bank work in London
to the great inconvenience of depositors? The work
could be much more expeditiously done in Ireland. Per-
haps some of the M.P.’s would do a little worrying «f
the P.M.G. on that matter. The Post Office people in
London can smile at these grievances unless they arc
troubled, Not having any experience of ‘‘the floor of the
Iouse ”’ we can offer no opinion as to the best way of
making it as nasty as possibly for the alien Post Office,
but could not the passing of the Post Office Estimates
he made as difficult as possible by the Irish members
until they get some satisfaction?

By the way, is it not a fact that the London pestal
service, which deals with a larger population than Ire-
land, is quite distinct from the provincial Post Office
service, and that all the superior appointments in it—
and they are presumably more numerous and more valu-
able than in Ireland—are given to London officials on
the recommendation of the controller?  Why should
not the Irish postal service be as distinct as that of
London, and why should not all superior Irish appoint-
ments go to officials serving in Ireland on the recom-
mendation of the authorities in Ireland similarly as is
done in connection with the London service? Perhaps
Mr. Field or Mr. Meagher would become curious on this
little matter.

Pity the poor officials of the Wrauthmines Urban
District Council!  They are so over-worked that per-
haps they don’t know whether they are standing on
their heads or on their heels. On the 6th May a resi-
dent house-owner got a demand note for the current

which the receipt was enclosed is in itself a criticism of

appears to have been addressed to ’samcw.here in a

Council Bill the Chief Sccretary thus referred to the particular
institution in which they wero interested. “Tho Council,’ he
said, ¢ discusses, tho Boards advise, and the Department does what
it, thinks best.” That was an absolutely correct statement of their
procedure up to tho present time, and, until some higher prin-
ciples of human conduct can be devised, he hoped tho Department
would continuo to do what it thinks best. But ho feared from the
cheers and laughter with which this admirablo stalement was
received (hat it gavo riso to the impression which, as thoy knew,
is wholly without foundation, that the Department did not regard
the wishes of the Council, and was not subject to the control of
the Boards. Ho stated positively, and he believed the Council
would be with him in every word that he said on this head, that
the officars enfrusted with the administrative functions of the
Department. had throughout endeavoured to carry out loyally the
wishes of the Council, and their deliberations had assisted in
bringing about results of permanent value.”

This is an admission that the Department takes the
nature of paternal government. We need not discuss
whether the Department had, or had not, endeavoured
to carry out the wishes of the Council ; the fact, in this
supposed democratic age, is that when all is said and
resoluted the Department does what </ thinks best. No
doubt even a dictatorship, were it a wise one, might be
fruitful of good rvesults, but dictatorships are out of
joint with the times; more than that, Sir Horace Plun-
kett and his coterie are, and with, we think, good
reason, distrusted by the people. ~We wonder what
would the ‘“poet’’ who edits the fresh-egg ‘‘ Home-
stead ’’ think of Cardinal Logue, for instance, if his
Eminence said that the people could discuss, others
advise, but that he and his friends would do what they
thought best. There are some people wha have a very
restricted sense of humour. They are violent partisans
first and humourists afterwards! Sir Horace Plunkett
has suffered, if he has also profited, by having a gang
of flatterers round him, and in addition to ‘‘the De-
partment "’ there has been a Department for exaggerat-
ing him into a hero, which he is not; and the fact that
the flattery of Sir Horace and of the Department as an
item in the Trish economy was, or appeared to be,
worked as an unofficial branch of the Department
naturally tended to create counter-feelings amongst the
people in TIreland. -

The Department under Sir Horace Plunkett, whether
it was Sir Horace Plunkett’s fault or not, was a dis-
appointment. We hope Mr. T. W. Russell will do
better. Of course, such a large Department with such
comparatively lavish resources could not fail to have
made some effect, and we are sure that those who drew
large salaries under it think that in some respects it
was one of the best things ever started in Ireland. We
hope_tlmt Mr. T. W. Russell will succeed in vastly in-
creasing its utility. e might try to do something
practical for existing ITrish manufacturers. One of the
great things that could be done, if the Department has
the legal power, is to keep a strict watch over Daddy
Siwindle Tingers, and put out detectives, and institute
prosecutions against anyone selling goods as Irish that
are not Irish. ¥

The decision of the Go~emm.ent . with ~-egard to the 
rate of payment for teaclnng Insh m National Schools,

as announced. by Mr. Cherry in the House of Commons

on Thursday, is that the grant per pupil to be given

in_schools in which Irish is taught as an ordinary sub-

ject shall be 3s., 6s., 9s. and 12s., respectively, for suc-
cessive courses, beginning in the third standard, and
that in the case of schools which adopt the bi-lingual

* programme the grant shall be 4s., 6s. or 8s. per pupil,
according to the merit shown by the school as a whole.
This scale of fees, whilst not exactly on the lines
demanded by the Gaelic League, which provided for
payment in all standards, is still regarded as fairly
‘satisfactory. The scale offered in the upper standards
is considered sufficiently liberal to induce teachers to
commence teaching Irish in the junior standards so as
to make sure of the fees later on. It is regarded as
absolutely essential that the teaching of Irish on oral
lines by means of object lessons, pictures and simple
conversation should begin as soon as the child enters
school if he is to grow up bi-lingual, which is the object
aimed at.

The new situation was carefully discussed at the fort-
nightly meeting of the Education Committee of the
Gaelic League on Friday, the 17th inst., and the fol-
lowing programme for schools was recommended and
ordered to be sent to the National Education Board :—
Infants.—To know the names-of at least twelve common
objects, and be able to build simple sentences vound
them. 1st Standard—In addition to foregoing, to know
the names of twenty common objects, and be able to
build sentences round them, also to be able to recognise
the simpler words of their vocabulary when written or
printed. 2nd Standard—In addition to foregoing, first
45 pages of ““ An Modh Dircach,” or first four lessons
of ‘“An Mac-Leighinn,” and to be able to recognise
the words of their vocabulary, and to read short sen-
tences when written or printed. 3rd Standard—(a)
All Part I. and up to page 33, Part IT., of ‘“ An Modh
Direach,’’ or first nine lessons of “ An Mac-Leighinn ;"
(b) Part I. «“ Ceachta Beaga Gaedhilhe,”” or ¢ An Chead

and () writing headlines. 4th Standard—(a) All Parts
1. and II. “An Modh Direach,”” or the first fifteen
lessons of ‘“ An Mac-Leighinn;”’ (b) a story of about
2,000 words; (¢) memorising of thirty lines of poetry
and transcription from text. 5th Standard—(a) To
read, understand and explain in Irish approved readers
or stories of about 15,000 words; (b) to memorise 50
lines of poetry; (c) dictation from text (d) easy conver-
sation. 6th Standard—(a) To read fluently, understand

The Education Committee of the Gaelic League,
Dublin, will be happy to give advice and co-operate
with managers of schools and teachers on these or any
other points connected with the teaching of Trish in the
schools. The new arrangement will be some relief to
the exchequer of the Gaelic League, as travelling
teachers will, under this scheme, probably receive suffi-
cient remuneration between the results fees for Irish
teaching in the schools and the subscriptions of the local
branches to obviate the necessity of the Coisde Gnotha
stepping in to finance them to such an extent as has
heen necessary during the past year. The Education
Committee decided to take steps to press on the Govern-
ment that the new scale of fees-be made apply to the
current year if possible, as the old scale has been
admitted on all sides to be hopelessly inadequate.

We have been favoured with copies of ““ The Garden
Manual ”’ and ““ The Trish Farmers’ Manual,”’ two pub-
lications of special interest respectively to those in-
terested in gardening and the larger field of agriculture.
““The Garden Manual’’ and ‘‘ Farmers’ Manual ’’ are
issued by Sir James W. Mackey, Ltd., the well-known
Dublin seed firm, and we are glad to add they are
printed and published in Ireland.

The annual meeting in aid of St. Joseph's Catholic
Boy’s Brigade Organisation will be held in the Boys’
Brigade Hall, Church St., on Sunday next. The chair
will be taken by the Lord Mayor at 3 p.m., and the
meeting will be addressed by several speakers.

We are asked to state that the last meeting of Pro-
testant Gaels was held on Saturday, May 18th, in the
rooms of Cpaod na gCirg sCuigy, 7 St. Stephen’s
Green. The principal business of the evening was the
placing of the movement on a satisfactory basis, so that
the work to be done should be carried out in a business-
like manner. It was resolved that those present should
constitute a committee with power to add to their num-
ber, to be called ‘“ Committee of Gaelic Leaguers for

THOUGHTS ON THE PRESENT SITUAIION.

Y the time this issue of the LEADER is in the

hands of the public the Convention will have

made its decision. It will have kicked out the “‘Liberal”’

proposals, or will have instructed the Parliamentary

Party to do their best with the Bill, subject to certain

amendments. (Since this article has been sent to the
printers the Convention has kicked out the Bill).

The argument against attempting to amend the Bill
appears to be that the Lords would reduce the Bill to
at least its original form if they let it pass at all.
Therefore, it is argued, as we would not take the
Bill unamended, kick out the ‘‘ Liberal ’’ proposals at
the Convention. But if it is not possible to force an
amended Bill through the Lords, what is it possible to
do by not attempting it, and throwing out the Bill at
the Convention?

If the Bill will have been kicked out at the Conven-
tion, the idea of pressing it forward in an amended
form being scouted, then the only course would” appear
to be to kick out the ‘‘ Liberals’’ and bring back the
Tories as soon as possible. For if the ‘‘ Liberals’’
brought in a new and less stringent Bill it
would be merely the ¢ Liberal”” Bill amended
by force of the Irish popular voice, and
the Lords would presumably reduce it to the attenuated
dimensions of the first Bill. Of course, it may be pos-
sible before the ‘‘ Liberals’’ are put out to get a few
small reforms through. But when the ‘ Liberals’’
are kicked out and the Tories are put in, are we to
expect that the Tories will be more liberal than the
‘“ Liberals?”’ It seems to us that if the Bill is kicked
out by the Convention that the Parliamentary Party,
as a leading national force, gets its quietus by the same
kick.

For our part we look, and have looked, on the Party
as a mere division of the national forces for the disband-
ing of which division there was no case made out. If
the Party are voted helpless in relation to this Bill the
relative importance of the Party diminishes almest to
vanishing point, and the national forces must face the
situation with the Party almost blurred out of the
picture. 5

If the Bill is kicked out by the Convention it is diffi-
cult to answer the question—what next?—except in
negatives. We have seen that one of Mr. Martyn’s gro-
tesque following has suggested that the Party made a
mistake in pressing the “ Liberals ’’ for this Bill rather
than in pressing them to go on with the University
matter. That involves a problem in Parliamentary
tactics that we will not now stay to consider; we only
note that, characteristically enough, this grotesque fol-
lower of Mr. Martyn has written—or rather spoken—
himself down a Parliamentarian. If an arrangement
concerning University education is desirable, and if the
Party might have got it by side-tracking other matters
than: out of the mouth of no less a person than Alder-
man Cole, we have evidence that the Parliamentary
Party are of potential use to Ireland! :

What we may call the psychological value of going
on—if possible—with the present proposals duly
amended deserve a fow minutes’ consideration.  Going
on with the Bill, and bringing such veforms as we can

214

to fruition will supply a new hope; and to us—a people
without much power over our own destiny—a new hope
is a recurring necessity if we are not to flop into general
apathy. The Irish revival was a new hope, and it
truly worked what many might consider miracles. We
have a recollection that years ago an eminent and
famous Irishman asked doubtingly if we thought that
the language would ever be brought back to the state
of being a general language of the country. Gathering
that we were speaking to a man who believed that it
would not, we contented ourselves with saying that even
if the language plank failed that the effort to revive
it, and all that that effort involved, would be to the
good. The language movement had supplied a much-
needed new hope to a country suffering from the effects
of an old political blasted hope.

We have often resented the view that we Irish are
fickle and lacking in perseverance. We don’t believe
a word of it. We display a seeming lack of persever-
ance because we have so little power that our efforts
do not produce commensurate effects, there is not
enough success, and so there is an inevitable reaction
against effort. There is another reason, though it pro-
perly arises out of the first—we have got into the habit
of attempting the impracticable; and no man, be he
stolid German, canny Scot, or so-called volatile Irish-
man, can pursue the impracticable without giving up
after a while.

One of the great facts in Ireland is that we lack
power over our own fortunes. ‘‘ Reiterating our
demands ’’ becomes tiresome after a time, and rather
farcical in the long run. To keep up real steam the
country must, to put it at the least, have something
tangible to play with.

If this Bill collapses, what will happen in the wake of
a fresh blasted hope? There may be a brief fever fol-
lowed by apathy and Anglicisation. Anglicisation, it
must be borne in mind, is for most the line of least re-
sistance, and acquiescence in the Union for many is the
easier course. Irish nationality is a fight up-hill in an age
when people find it hard emough to progress on the
level. If our political hopes are dashed to the ground
and smashed, every thinking Irish Nationalist may well
feel anxious for the future. ~Bubbles of froth may
mark the eruption of adjectives and tall talk on the
part of Mr. Edward Martyn and Co., but they will
raise no storm and swamp no empires. John Sweet-
man, it may be confidently predicted, will not ascend
the gallows, and no Aughrim dog will prove his fidelity
by the side of the corpse of Edward Martyn. Unless
vigorous action is taken by the United Irish League,
the Parliamentary Party, and such really popular
forces, we fear there will be a reaction towards Anglici-
sation; ‘“ tame’’ Catholics will spread, emigration and
general national slump will develop. That certainly is
a dismal picture. ~Mr. Nannetti and other ‘‘ Labour
leaders”’ will weave closer the bonds between West
British and British workingmen, and the Trades Iall
in Capel Street may be called Little Manchester.  All
Ireland will tend to become a Humphrey’s Dump, and
the ‘“ Independent’’ will attempt to be its organ. If
we cannot get power, may Ieaven send us a new hope
to keep up nationality as a going concern.

If the Bill is rejected there is mno use asking the
country to transfer their hopes to the Tories when, by
the aid of the Irish Party, the ““ Liberals’’ may be
kicked from office. The United Irish League, the Irish
Party, ‘and all such political forces as are gathered
round these bodies must face the issue that the kicking
of the Bill sounds the practical death-knell of Parlia-
mentary action for a time—at least; the old nation-
making forces, the language and industrial revival, the
fight for tolerance and fair play, must go on, but a new
hope will have to be supplied by the Parliamentary

forces completely shifting their helm, and fighting and
working at home, making Government impossible.

THE DIGNITY OF LABOUR.

\ N 7 B are accustomed from time to time to hear
high-sounding _praises of the Dignity and
Nobility of Labour. There are many classes who in-
duge in such praises; but few there are who by their
actions proclaim their real belief in it. In this, as in
all things clse, actions speak louder than words: but in
the mouths of the majority the phrase ‘‘ Dignity and
nobility of labour’ is but a mere shibboleth—mere
empty bombast. And yet how often we hear it, and
how lightly it is used. ~We hear it spoken of by
*“ Weary Willies,”” who have only a nodding acquaint-
ance with labour, and would avoid it altogether if they
only could. It is also at times indulged in by the
demagogue taken from the labour classes who hopes
that, by the opportunities which his ability to speak—
the glib tongue so often the handmaid of the grama-
phonic mind—will furnish him with, he may raise him-
self above his fellows to a proud eminence from which
he may despise the dignity and nobility of labour as in
easy aflluence he will hold forth incessantly in its praise.
“Such be your gods, oh, Israel!’”’ Again, we have
the dilettante—the superior person who holds forth in
general and generous terms. You know him: the man
with the cast of features of a masculing woman, with
long snaky locks, lily-white hands, and collar and tie
that suggest the opal hush. e waves his hand, and
in his condescending fashion lectures you on the dignity
and nobility of labour. Perchance you are a labourer
in the field trying to force from a too churlish soil your
scanty daily bread, earning it all too literally by the
sweat of your brow. It may be that you toil mentally
and physically in some city, in the noxious atmosphere
of a congested factory. Your blood poisoned with every
breath you draw, and your physical endurance
diminished almost to vanishing point as you toil, day in
and day out, hoping to receive, not what you earn—
employers are no more inclined to be just than other
men—bhut what your employer has forced you to accept,
and what is perhaps only sufficient to enable you to
keep starvation from the door of your home—the home
your work permits you so little to enjoy.  Along
comes our ultra-refined, wsthetic lecturer to tell you
of the dignity and nobility of labour. Will not the
recording angel blot out with a tear that curse you
grind between your teeth as the fool turns and goes his
way leaving but bitterness behind? And yet labour
is noble and dignified when rightly viewed. Just as it
is the man that adorns the position and not the position
the man, so the dignity that is in the labour is the
dignity the toiler londs it. The noble man ever does
noble work, because he himself is noble. The position
which an undignified man holds loses that dignity which
his predecessor had given it. When one of the Roman
Iimperors made his horse a Consul, he realised this to
perfoction. Burns has wisely said: ““ The rank is but
the guinea stamp; the man’s the gold for all that.”
Goldsmith, towards the close of ‘‘The Deserted Vil-
lage,”” says much the same.
Now, in order that a toiler may, under the present in-
cquitable conditions, realise his dignity and nobility, he
must look out beyond himself and comprehend, obscurely

AN ECHO OF THE PAST. 
T
BE article dealing with Mr. Dickie's attitude 
towards the Irish language in the Wexford dis-
trict, by ",vexfordman," in a recent issue of the 
LEADER carried me back a dozen years. It was, indeed, 
a pleasant retrospect. 
I reviewed in my ·mind all the 
joys and aspirations of the budding pedagogue ; for in 
f~ncy I again revelled in my teens, and in the happy 
days of my monitorial course in an Irish National 
School. 
Had I the wisdom and foresight to have kept 
a diary, after the fashion of " ,villiam O'Brien from 
Mallow town," and lesser celebrities, what amazing and 
amusing episodes might I unfold. 
However, there is a 
receptacle of past incidents far more portable than paper 
or parchment records-it is the human memory. 
Of the period of which I write there are impressed on 
my mind events that time and distance can never efface. 
My recollections of District Inspector Dickie-for such 
he was then-are foremost amongst them. 
As his name 
has been brought to the notice of readers of the LEADER, 
perhaps it would not constitute an intrusion on my part 
to chronicle a few of my experiences with that estim-
able, though un-Irish, gentleman. 
A! the Irish lan-
guage has been associated with the actions of this school 
inspector I had better deal firstly with the point iu my 
reminiscences touching thereon. 
It was in the days of the Results Fee regime. 
The 
Fourth Class were being examined in Agriculture. The 
text-book on the subject was by Baldwin. 
Mr. Dickie 
plied the class with questions on the planting of the 
potato-a very appropriate subject, indeed, for the boys, 
the staple diet of whom was represented by 
that 
"flowery" tuber. 
Let me slightly diverge to say that 
Mr. Dickie was a very fair examiner; but he had a 
most original way of framing his questions. 
Unlike 
,other inspectors with whom I came in contact his mode 
of questioning never suggested to the children the cor-
rect form of reply. 
His questions were invariably put 
in such a manner as to make the pupil think indepen-
•dently, and use his intelligence in framing the answer 
-in language of his own-not to reply parrot-like in the 
words of the book. In this particular instance the in-
:apector was endeavouring to get from the class the name 
·applied by Baldwin to the potato-or part ,thereof-
·placed in the ground as " seed." 
The exact word he 
-sought for as the correct answer was " sets;" but not 
one of the boys eeeml)d 11eady to supply it. After a <long 
·time ane of the lade raised his hand to signal his ability 
to reply. 
"Well," said .Dickie, "what do you eal'l the 
-potato put into the ground?" ·"Sc10Lt-<,n, sir," shouted 
;the youth, who .strangely enough l1appened to be my 
younger brother. 
Mr. Dickie was nonplussed-be ~t 
was who eventually got cornered by the reply to bxs 
own question. 
He turned to the teacher for an inter-
pretation of the strange word. 
The explanation given 
apparently satisfied the fastidious examiner, for he 
placed a number one pass to the credit of my brother. 
The answer was intelligent and absolutely correct, even 
though couched in the tongue of the " foul, nefarious· 
Gael;" and Mr. Dickie had to be satisfied. 
To give 
him his due he manfully rewarded the boy for replying 
in his mother tongue, for he asked the young hero no 
further questions. 
Thus by a stroke of the pen In-
spector Dickie authorised payment for the use of one 
Irish word. 
He then unconsciously admitted his belief 
in fees for Irish; now he has evidently changed his 
opinions. It is a pity. Into what a living flame may 
not such a smoulderirig ember burst forth were it fanned 
as by Mr. Dickie at that happy moment years ago!

K FRENCH YIEW OF THE IRISH QUEST:ON.*

: IN' defining the attitude of M. Dubois towards the
Irish Question, I think I convey a generally cor-
rect improssion by saying (as has been said of our some-
what gay-hearted Chief Secretary) that.‘“ he has fallen
in love with his Dark Rosaleen.”” I think further that,
romembering our author’s fervent sympathy with Irish
aspirations, Dark Rosaleen would, on acquaintance, re-
ciprocate the affection. There may, of course, exist the
cloments of an occasional trifling difference which would
conceivably lead to a ‘‘ tiff ’ in the courtship ; but then,
““the course of true love did never yet run smooth,”
and whilo the hypersensitive Rosaleen might, now and
then feel her sentiments a little offended I am' confident
that the consequent irritation would never eventuate in
& permanent estrangement.

All this, T confess, does not represent with faultless
precision the opinions of our French admirer. ‘‘The
trath is "’—to use one of his own pet phrases—he can-
not conveniently be dropped into any of the familiar
categories in Irish Politics. A Unionist he emphati-
cally is not; and, though in a large measure he is a
Nationalist, T hesitate to give him that designation as
he is not always a too friendly critic of orthodox
Nationalism. How then shall we label Monsieur? I
think we can get out of the difficulty only by doing M.
Dubois the honour of coining a name for his political
christening—let us call him an Irish-Ireland Home
Ruler.

Coming to closer quarters with our friend we find
his work to be a critical study of the Irish question from:
every standpoint—historical, political, social, economic,.
etc. No phase of the complex situation escapes his:
notice, and his treatment of the more important sub-
jects reveals a deep and accurate knowledge of their-
related circumstances and conditions. And what, you.
may ask, in the first place, is the opinion of M. Dubois
concerning the causes whicli have operated towards the-:
birth of the many ills which we see in Ireland to-day?
A sentence of Dr. Sigerson, in his recent admirable:
lecture on ‘‘ The Peasants of the West,”” furnishes us:
with an answer to this question:

Dr. Sigerson’s words are—‘‘ He cannot understand’
the present who ignores the past.”” So, too, says M.
Dubois: ““ The past alone explains the present.” It is-
in the history of the past that we find the most power-
tul cause of those great evils that affect our national’
welfare. The Penal Laws, the misgovernment, the in-
justice, all have combined to retard the progress and’
break the spirit of the Irish people. The genius, the-
soul of the nation, has been crushed to the point of
extinction, hence our intellectual decay, our lethargy,
our slavishness. ‘“In Ireland,”’ says M. Dubois, ‘‘ the
worst danger is the mental and moral decadence, and
this decadence, let me hasten to add, is, for a great:
part, the result of oppression.’’

Turning to our social condition he asks what obstacles
have impeded its development. The cause of this un-
progressiveness, he tells us, ‘‘is manifestly in the his-
tory, such as England has made it, of Ireland during
seven centuries—seven centuries of raids and of wars,
of massacres, of invasions, and of persecutions. Do we .
not easily understand the frightful and destructive in-
fluence of such a past on the Irish spirit.”” To our
history he again recurs for an explanation of those
alleged defects in our character which other political
philosophers attribute to our Religion. ‘“ Careless and®
lazy, without dignity and witlioutself-control; they’ (the
Trish) ““lack energy in the material life and character in
the moral life: Such are the great faults with which they
arc most commonly reproached, but such are the very
faults which' reveal the effects of the past. No doubi:

there may be in this humid, soft climate, in this ‘seda-
‘tive soporific’ air something depressing, which enervates
‘the spirit, and explains these easy manners . . . this
strength of endurance but not of enterprise, . . . but
there is no doubt either that oppression has parti-
cularly aggravated the evil.” Such is the lesson which
our author reads from the past, let us now glance at his
Views concerning some aspects of the present.

In Irgland to-day there are, he tells us, two Irelands,
‘two nations: ‘“ Above, the Colony or British Garrison,
the newcomers, the last Planters, the privileged classes ;
below, ‘the people, the vanquished, the helots.” T is
Fn.ot so much a division of race, nor even of religion, the
line of demarcation is especially social and political.
“‘ National interests, national prejudices and national
aspirations separate the two Irelands more than race or
religion.” For the Garrison, as ropresented in politics
by Unionism, he has scant vespect. “‘ Politically, their
numerical weakness brings them to immobility, to in-
ertia. They have no men—no capable chiefs.  They
have not even a programme, or at least they have only
@ negative programme, that is, to oppose the Iome
Rule desires of National Ireland, and to this principle
of opposition they have never béen able to add a con-
structive idea, or an idea of progress.  Their policy
reduces itself to exciting the passions of the British
Celtophobe, under the pretext of enlightening British
public opinion, and of denouncing violently in the Eng-
lish Press Nationalism and the Nationalists.”” Further,
““ somewhat ashamed before the world of bearing an
Trish name, they glorify themselves with being ‘ British

_ citizens,” with being ‘ loyalists * and the only ‘loyalists
in Ireland: Still, this loyalism is conditional and sub-
ject to their enjoyment of Government favours, it is
superficial loyalism—loyalism® of the pocket; it is
fidelity, not to the British Constitution, but to what
they consider is owed to them by England.” Again,
““ Unionists, they make war on the Government when
it is Liberal, because it is then friendly to the
Nationalists; they do likewise even if the Government
is Unionist and is lukewarm for their cause.  They
strive for the policy of Coercion as against the policy of
reparation, and they demand compensation for their
lost privileges. Strange state of mind is that of these
ultra-Unionists—more royal than the King and more
imperialist and reactionary than their English friends
and brothers!’’ This will be unpalatable reading for
Mr. Wm. Moore and his associates, who just now can
find no better means of displaying their rancorous
bigotry than by vilifying their country before the
House of Commons.

M. Dubois, as may be scen, has a fine scorn for the
Unionists, but it should not be inferred that he regards
the Nationalists as above criticism. He does unot
attempt to palliate a fault no matter in what party he
finds it, and this impartiality contributes materially to
the value of his work. Iaving paid a tribute to the
Nationalists for their unfailing integrity and courage,
he goes on to say: ‘“ They have been without fear; have
‘they been without reproach?’’ He thinks not.  Their
irreconcilability is to him a grave defect in their policy.
Why, he asks, cannot they respond to conciliation by
conciliation, although they may not for the time gain
cverything they desire? Hven without abandoning any
of their principles, why do they not frankly accept
every offer that marks a step in the right direction?
““They forget somewhat that perfection is not to be
attained here below, and that this world is made up of
compromises and half-satisfactions.”  But the most
serious error of Nationalism he states thus—‘‘ Nation-
alists have seen in the Irish Question only Politics ; and
in Politics only agitation and Home Rule. Of the
complexity of the social life they appear not to be very
conscious: the intellectual, moral and cconomic pro-
gross of the country, the education of the democracy—

‘they have neglected all that. TFor them, all the evil
.came from England, from the Union. It is to England,
they say, that Ireland must look for every reform; it is
through Flome Rule only that Ireland can be saved.
With Mome Rule all will go well ; without it, there is
o hope.” Does not this read like a passage from ‘‘ The

of historical causes, other agencies are at work operating
in the same direction. Amongst such impeding factors,,

ET me commence by expressing my indebtedness

to ‘ Eireannac’’ for the pains he has taken in

the LeapEr of May the 11th and May the 18th to

define the rights of the Church in the matter of
Primary Education.

T agree with ‘“ Eireannac’’ that the Church has ever
been solicitous about the education—secular and reli-
gious—of her children. For ages the world was in-
debted to her for education and civilization; and in
these countries a National system of Primary Education
endowed by the State is only of comparatively recent
origin. Tn missionary countries, after the chapel, the
school is still the most anxious object of the Church’s
solicitude ; and, ncedless to say, she is the Manager of
her own schools, she appoints the teachers, determines
the subjects to be taught, and selects the school books.

But is there no distinction to be drawn between
schools established by the Church herself and schools
cstablished by the State? Granted that the Church
can manage, through her priests, the schools that are
her own, does it follow that where you have a National
system of education, the lay authority can claim only
the privilege of paying, and that the Church must have
the right of appointing the teachers and of determining
the whole course of secular instruction? I have had
my own children educated privately by a tutor; could
my Rector have claimed the exclusive right of appoint-
ing and dismissing the tutor to my family, and of pre-
seribing a course of secular instruction for my children ?
There are in England—-and in Ireland, too—Primary
and Secondary Schools established and owned by private
persons, but open to the public; have the owners of
these schools the right of appointing their teachers and
of exercising general management of the schools? or
shall we say that the Rectors or Parish Priests can claim
the n.xcllusivc right, in the name of faith and morals, of
appointing teachers in these schools and of determining
the programme of secular instruction? ** Eireannac’’
has quite ncedlessly gone into the historic relation of
the Church—with her own schools; for no ono ever
quostioned the exclusive right of the Church to manage
the schools which she builds and maintains herself. But
in dealing with this historic aspect of the question he
appears to have missed the main issue of this discus-
sion ; viz., has the Church the exclusive right of manag-
ing schools established and maintained by the State?

In the Leaper of May 18th  Eireannac’’ tells us
that it is not to the Canons of Dionysius Exiguus or
Mercator, or to the Decretals of Gregory IX., we must
go to 1e:u'.n.our duties in the matter of education, but
Lo the decisions of our Bishops ; their decisions—he tells
us—constitute the Canon Law which Catholics are
bound to obey.

often intended—to convey an idea that the man who

whereas his knowledge may all be derived from an
Encyclopedia at his back, or a moderate selection of
books of reference on his shelves.

And now for rhapsody No. 2: —

ing of her—although I was often .absorbed in my work—although

in all this. The everlasting hills are but a few days older! I did

realised, novertheless, that it was profoundly dangerous. I I?II.

T might quote a lot more of the same nature, but
what I have given will, T fancy, suffice. Is there not
something repulsive to us, Trish Catholics, in this asso-
ciation, almost in the same breath, of illicit love and
religious devotion, of those sublime rhapsodies quoted
by your correspondent and that erotic one just given by
me, and all of which emanate from the same individual.
To me there is something bordering on profanity, if not
indeed on sacrilege, in the association. But, doubtless,
the light in which one would view the matter would
depend on one’s temperament modified by the surviving
influences of the moral and religious environment of
one’s earlier days.

1 am not saying, nor do T want to maintain, that such
novels 'as ““ The School for Saints’’ may not be read
with pleasure, if not with profit, by cultured Catholic
men (I would draw the line at women—pure Irish
Catholic women at all events) like your correspondent,
who would appreciate them for their philosophical and
psychological and literary excellences alone. But the
typical patrons of the Parochial or Free Library—in
fact, ninety-nine in every one hundred borrowers—have
but the haziest conception—if any at all—of what
philosophy and psychology mean. Their interest—and
I have had peculiar opportunities of forming an opinion
on the point—would centre altogether on the plot
and the concrete action of the characters. They .
would, as I said in my previous article, just skip
those beautiful rhapsodies which so captivate your cor-
respondent. And even if they read them they would,
with the strong simple faith and delicate moral per-
ception of the bulk of them, be more scandalised than
cdified when they remembered that the convert, Orange,
who gave expression to these sublime religious rhap-

the novel, confessed in an almost shameless fashion his
love for another man’s wife-—a love, too, which, instead
of regretting or attempting to repress, he sought rather
to excuse.

And I do not think it could elevate our Church in the
cstimation of outsiders when they found that one, like
Orange, who had so recently entered i, had not at the
same time completely severed the chain which bound
him to this illicit love. Using a very homely simile,
and likening ‘‘ The School for Saints’’ to a barrel of
apples, it has very few fruit that are not discased or
tainted ; and T do not believe that any of our rustics—

Just as in the material world there are great dangers
associated with the hunt for gold and pearls and pre-
clous stones generally—dangers of the primitive wilds
of the deep, of the Stock Exchanges and the crowded
marts, so also in the search for gems of literature and
philosophy the perils to be encountered are of no less
magnitude. These gems are, too often, buried deep
down, under most objectionable literary niuck, in the
works of sensual poets and novelists, or materialistic
essayists and philosophers, and our typical Parochial or
